title: "Radicalized" About AI.
description:
I explain my transformation from AI skeptic to believer. In this video I include five key reasons why I believes AI disruption is inevitable. Drawing on insights from friends at major tech companies and massive investment data, I argue that the writing is on the wall for radical change and share my preparation strategy.

If you want to grow a newsletter faster, try Newsletter Hero AI. Use code BETA45 for more than 50 percent off the monthly plan https://www.newsletterhero.ai/ 

Key Insights

Tech insiders know something we don't - People working at the biggest AI companies are privately preparing for massive disruption while publicly staying quiet to protect their jobs and income.

Historical patterns repeat - AI mirrors the electricity revolution: impressive but impractical early stages followed by complete societal reorganization once the "light bulb moment" hits.

The investment scale is unprecedented - $1.6 trillion invested in AI since 2013, with $5.2 trillion more needed by 2030 for data centers alone.

Zero-cost preparation strategy - Preparing for AI disruption through building businesses, creating content, and developing resilient skills has no downside even if the timeline is wrong.


🐤My Twitter - https://twitter.com/KenJee_DS
👔 LinkedIn -  https://www.linkedin.com/in/kenjee/

title: How I Built a Profitable AI Product in 43 Days
description:
I built my SaaS Newsletter Hero in 43 days in order to prepare for the massive AI disruption I see coming. This was my process, the lows and the highs. 

Check it out here: https://www.newsletterhero.ai/ 
Code: BETA45 ($99 for the full year - more than 50% off the monthly rate)

00:45 Days 1-10: Foundation & Planning - Team organization took unexpected energy and time - Multiple decisions required before writing any code - Key lesson: "Working with a human team forced accountability"

02:15 Days 11-24: Market Research & MVP  - Newsletter market chosen after extensive analysis - Three core pain points identified through creator interviews: - Writing faster in personal voice - Stronger subject lines - Easier multi-platform distribution - [03:45] MVP features: custom templates, subject line generation, user profiles, credits, payments

05:30 Days 25-35: Launch Reality Check - Major assumption proven wrong: Users wanted control, not one-click solutions -  Marketing became unexpectedly difficult despite existing audience - Multiple integrations added: Kit, Mailchimp, podcast uploads - Key insight: "Building a product and getting people to care about a product are two completely different games"

07:45 Day 35: First Paying Customer  - Customer acquired through Reddit comment on micro-SaaS celebration post - Validation moment: Stranger paid for the product - Customer feedback immediately integrated, especially onboarding improvements

09:15 Days 36-43: Growth & Learning - Additional paying customers from existing network - Product responsibility realization as users depend on it for income - Honest admission: "I am currently just ass at marketing"

title: Why I’m Building a SaaS (before AI takes over)
description:
I'm starting over. I'm building a SaaS before AI takes over. 

I believe we have a 1.5 - 2 year window where technical skills are still a valuable advantage for creating. I'm going to capitalize on this golden window with my new company Newsletter hero. 

My goal is to get good at building and scaling companies in this window. I would like to spin up 3-5 before we face the mass uncertainty that AI brings. 

After that, I'm buying a farm! Like/ Subscribe if you want to join me there :)

Follow my Journey Here:

title: How to Get Transcripts from AI with Whisper & DaVinci Resolve Step by Step Guide
description:
Learn how to easily generate AI transcripts for YouTube videos using OpenAI’s Whisper API and DaVinci Resolve. In this step-by-step tutorial, I’ll show you how to split audio into one-minute chunks, process them through Whisper, and use the transcripts to boost your SEO with titles, hashtags, and more.

This method is perfect if you want to:

Improve YouTube search rankings with full transcripts.

Save time and money with free tools like DaVinci Resolve.

Get better suggestions from AI for titles, tags, and descriptions.

💡 Cost example: About $0.08 for 3 minutes of video (prices vary).
⚡ Bonus tip: Automate multiple audio chunks on Windows for faster processing.

👉 Stick around to the end for the exact export settings I used in DaVinci Resolve to make Whisper work smoothly.

0:00 – Why transcripts help SEO & YouTube growth
0:25 – Using GPT for SEO title + hashtag ideas
0:35 – Whisper API for transcription (limitations & timeouts)
1:03 – Fix: Splitting video/audio into 1-minute chunks in DaVinci Resolve
1:31 – Exporting audio clips (MP3 settings)
2:10 – Cost breakdown & final workflow
2:22 – Running Whisper API on all chunks at once

#YouTubeSEO #WhisperAI #DaVinciResolve #AITranscripts #contentcreation 

Full Transcript (auto-caption backup):
Hey everybody, just a quick note on how to get audio transcripts easily from an AI. 
I have been wanting to increase the views of my YouTube videos so I asked GPT for 
some SEO suggestions. One thing it suggested which is both helpful for search engines and nice was to 
put a transcript to everybody was to put a transcript with each video. The other good thing this does
is when you have a transcript, you can hand it to GPT and GPT can suggest a good title and hashtags 
and all that kind of good SEO stuff.
So, I needed a transcript and I work with AIs all the time and so the first thing that occurred to me was that I had a 
command line tool called that Simon Willison wrote and that it interacted with 
the OpenAI API and had something called in uses OpenAI's Whisper API, awesome. 
So I went off, I asked that to make me a transcript, that's what Whisper can do, and it 
kept timing out over and over and over. That is when I surmised, and I was correct, I looked it up, 
that Whisper likes about one minute chunks of audio at a time. And so this is where DaVinci Resolve comes in. 
DaVinci Resolve is a free download video editor, and I went into DaVinci Resolve and I split my audio, or my video, 
with the audio into one minute chunks using breaks, or Ctrl-B. So I went Ctrl-B, Ctrl-B, Ctrl-B. Then 
I went to the delivery section and turned off video export, told it I did want to export audio. I made sure that I...
I'm that this is mp3 and mp3 and they matched went to the file section said I wanted individual clips 
So now each break is going to be an individual clip Told it to use custom names. So they're easy to work with 
And told it to use unique file names. So my custom name is going to come from up here And lo and behold 
I got the entire transcript out there it is just so you know As far as I know, the cost was about 
$0.08 for about 3 minutes of video For me, on this weekend. I know the costs change over time 
Now, for folks like me that are stuck on Windows You can have it work on all the blocks at once 
Have the LLM Whisper API work on all the blocks at once You have to jump through a few hoops 
You have to tell it to do something
Do something like this which which gets all the mp3s and then passes it through this for api 
And that is all thanks everybody. Have a good day

title: How Attention Mechanism Works in Transformer Architecture
description:
#llm #embedding #gpt 
The attention mechanism in transformers is a key component that allows models to focus on different parts of an input sequence when making predictions. Attention assigns varying degrees of importance to different parts of the input, enabling the model to capture contextual relationships effectively. The most widely used form of attention in transformers is self-attention, where each token in a sequence attends to all other tokens, capturing long-range dependencies. This mechanism is further enhanced by multi-head attention, which enables the model to focus on multiple aspects of the data simultaneously.

There are several types of attention mechanisms, including self-attention, which is used in transformers to relate different words in the same sentence, and cross-attention, which is commonly seen in tasks like machine translation where the model attends to a separate input sequence. 

In the video, I have visually explained each of these attention mechanisms with clear animations and step-by-step breakdowns.

Timestamps:

0:00 - Embedding and Attention
2:12 - Self Attention Mechanism
10:52 - Causal Self Attention
14:12 - Multi Head Attention
16:50 - Attention in Transformer Architecture
17:54 - GPT-2 Model
21:30 - Outro

Attention is all you need paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762

Music by Vincent Rubinetti
Download the music on Bandcamp:
https://vincerubinetti.bandcamp.com
Stream the music on Spotify:
https://open.spotify.com/artist/2SRhEEt2tlDQWxzwfUo9Dl

title: LM Studio Tutorial: Run Large Language Models (LLM) on Your Laptop
description:
In this video, I'll show you how to run large language models (LLMs) right on your laptop using LM Studio! LM Studio uses GPU offloading to break down complex AI models, allowing you to run them smoothly on almost any laptop. With LM Studio accelerated by NVIDIA RTX GPUs, you unlock faster performance and better handling of larger models, giving you power and speed for advanced workflows. Whether you're generating text, summarizing documents, or asking questions, I'll guide you through the installation process, how to load and use models like Llama and Gemma, and even how to upload files for analysis—all while keeping your data secure on your PC. Perfect for those who want to explore AI without relying on cloud services!

Host: Kevin Stratvert

title: EASIEST Way to Fine-Tune a LLM and Use It With Ollama
description:
In this video, we go over how you can fine-tune Llama 3.1 and run it locally on your machine using Ollama! We use the open source repository "Unsloth" to do all of the fine tuning on an SQL dataset!

Throughout this video, we discuss the ins and outs of what fine-tuning an LLM is, how you can work with your data so that the LLM can process it and then importing it into Ollama for you to run locally on your machine!

title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step | Deep Learning Chapter 6
description:
Demystifying attention, the key mechanism inside transformers and LLMs.
Instead of sponsored ad reads, these lessons are funded directly by viewers: https://3b1b.co/support
Special thanks to these supporters: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/attention#thanks
An equally valuable form of support is to simply share the videos.

Demystifying self-attention, multiple heads, and cross-attention.
Instead of sponsored ad reads, these lessons are funded directly by viewers: https://3b1b.co/support

The first pass for the translated subtitles here is machine-generated and, therefore, notably imperfect. To contribute edits or fixes, visit https://www.criblate.com

Звуковая дорожка на русском языке: Влад Бурмистров.

------------------

Here are a few other relevant resources

Build a GPT from scratch, by Andrej Karpathy
https://youtu.be/kCc8FmEb1nY

If you want a conceptual understanding of language models from the ground up, @vcubingx just started a short series of videos on the topic:
https://youtu.be/1il-s4mgNdI?si=XaVxj6bsdy3VkgEX

If you're interested in the herculean task of interpreting what these large networks might actually be doing, the Transformer Circuits posts by Anthropic are great. In particular, it was only after reading one of these that I started thinking of the combination of the value and output matrices as being a combined low-rank map from the embedding space to itself, which, at least in my mind, made things much clearer than other sources.
https://transformer-circuits.pub/2021/framework/index.html

Site with exercises related to ML programming and GPTs
https://www.gptandchill.ai/codingproblems

History of language models by Brit Cruise,  @ArtOfTheProblem  
https://youtu.be/OFS90-FX6pg

An early paper on how directions in embedding spaces have meaning:
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1301.3781.pdf

------------------

Timestamps:
0:00 - Recap on embeddings
1:39 - Motivating examples
4:29 - The attention pattern
11:08 - Masking
12:42 - Context size
13:10 - Values
15:44 - Counting parameters
18:21 - Cross-attention
19:19 - Multiple heads
22:16 - The output matrix
23:19 - Going deeper
24:54 - Ending

------------------

These animations are largely made using a custom Python library, manim.  See the FAQ comments here:
https://3b1b.co/faq#manim
https://github.com/3b1b/manim
https://github.com/ManimCommunity/manim/

All code for specific videos is visible here:
https://github.com/3b1b/videos/

The music is by Vincent Rubinetti.
https://www.vincentrubinetti.com
https://vincerubinetti.bandcamp.com/album/the-music-of-3blue1brown
https://open.spotify.com/album/1dVyjwS8FBqXhRunaG5W5u

------------------

3blue1brown is a channel about animating math, in all senses of the word animate. If you're reading the bottom of a video description, I'm guessing you're more interested than the average viewer in lessons here. It would mean a lot to me if you chose to stay up to date on new ones, either by subscribing here on YouTube or otherwise following on whichever platform below you check most regularly.

Mailing list: https://3blue1brown.substack.com
Twitter: https://twitter.com/3blue1brown
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/3blue1brown
Reddit: https://www.reddit.com/r/3blue1brown
Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/3blue1brown
Patreon: https://patreon.com/3blue1brown
Website: https://www.3blue1brown.com
